content.definition: Matrix multiplication is the operation that takes an m x n matrix A and an n x p matrix B and produces an m x p matrix C = AB, where each entry C(i,j) is the dot product of row i of A with column j of B.
content.layman: Matrix multiplication lets you combine two table-shaped transformations into one: the left matrix acts first, the right matrix second, and the product matrix captures doing both in a single step.
content.content:
## What is matrix multiplication?

Matrix multiplication combines two matrices A and B into a new matrix C = AB that represents applying B and then A as linear transformations.
If A has shape m x n and B has shape n x p, then AB has shape m x p, keeping the "outer" dimensions and requiring the inner dimensions to match.
Each entry C(i,j) is computed as the dot product of row i of A with column j of B, summing over the shared inner dimension.

### Shape rule (inner and outer dimensions)

The product AB is defined exactly when the number of columns of A equals the number of rows of B.
That shared size is the inner dimension; the result keeps the outer dimensions, so A in R^{m x n} and B in R^{n x p} give AB in R^{m x p}.
If the inner dimensions do not match, AB is undefined, even if both inputs are valid matrices.

### Row-by-column rule

To compute C(i,j), pick row i of A and column j of B, multiply corresponding entries and add them up.
Algebraically, C(i,j) = sum over k of A(i,k) * B(k,j), where k runs from 1 to the inner dimension n.
This row-by-column rule couples whole rows and columns, which is why changing one entry of A or B can affect an entire row or column of C.

### Matrix multiplication as composition of linear maps

An m x n matrix A represents a linear map from R^n to R^m, and an n x p matrix B represents a linear map from R^p to R^n.
The product AB represents first applying B and then applying A, giving a composite linear map from R^p to R^m.
This is why matrix multiplication is associative (A(BC) = (AB)C) but not commutative in general (AB and BA usually differ in both shape and effect).

### Identity matrices and special cases

The n x n identity matrix In has ones on the main diagonal and zeros elsewhere and acts as a multiplicative identity for n-column matrices.
For any m x n matrix A, AI_n = A and I_m A = A, provided the shapes are compatible.
These identities justify thinking of matrix multiplication as the algebra of composing linear transformations rather than as entrywise arithmetic.
keyPoints: AB is defined when columns(A) = rows(B); the result has shape rows(A) x columns(B).
Each entry C(i,j) in AB is the dot product of row i of A with column j of B.
Matrix multiplication corresponds to composing linear maps and is associative but not generally commutative.
Identity matrices act as multiplicative identities: AI = A and IA = A whenever the products are defined.

snippets.title: Hand-computing a small matrix product
snippets.explanation: Each entry of C is a dot product of a row of A with a column of B; this is the core row-by-column rule in a concrete 2 x 3 times 3 x 2 example.

snippets.title: Two-layer linear network as a product of weight matrices
snippets.code:
import numpy as np

batch_size = 64
input_dim = 128
hidden_dim = 256
output_dim = 10

X = np.random.randn(batch_size, input_dim)      # inputs, shape (64, 128)
W1 = np.random.randn(input_dim, hidden_dim)     # first layer weights, (128, 256)
W2 = np.random.randn(hidden_dim, output_dim)    # second layer weights, (256, 10)

# Standard forward pass
hidden = X @ W1         # shape (64, 256)
logits = hidden @ W2    # shape (64, 10)

# Equivalent single linear map using a pre-multiplied weight matrix
W_combined = W1 @ W2     # shape (128, 10)
logits2 = X @ W_combined

print(np.allclose(logits, logits2))  # True
snippets.explanation: Matrix multiplication composes linear layers: pre-multiplying weight matrices W1 and W2 gives a single combined weight matrix that produces the same outputs from X.

snippets.title: Checking shape compatibility before multiplying
snippets.code:
import numpy as np

A = np.random.randn(50, 100)
B = np.random.randn(100, 20)
C = np.random.randn(30, 50)

# AB is valid: inner dimensions 100 and 100 match
AB = A @ B          # shape (50, 20)

# BA is invalid because 20 != 50; this would raise a ValueError if attempted
if B.shape[1] != A.shape[0]:
    print("BA is undefined: inner dimensions", B.shape[1], "and", A.shape[0], "do not match")

# CA is valid: inner dimensions 50 and 50 match
CA = C @ A          # shape (30, 100)
print(AB.shape, CA.shape)
snippets.explanation: By inspecting shapes before multiplying, you can quickly see which products are defined and what shapes the results will have, avoiding trial-and-error with runtime errors.

snippets.title: Pure Python implementation of matrix multiplication
snippets.code:
from typing import List

Matrix = List[List[float]]

def matmul(a: Matrix, b: Matrix) -> Matrix:
    m, n = len(a), len(a[0])
    n2, p = len(b), len(b[0])
    if n != n2:
        raise ValueError(f"Incompatible shapes {m}x{n} and {n2}x{p} for matrix multiplication")

    # Initialise result with zeros
    c: Matrix = [[0.0 for _ in range(p)] for _ in range(m)]

    # Standard triple-loop algorithm
    for i in range(m):          # rows of A
        for j in range(p):      # columns of B
            s = 0.0
            for k in range(n):  # inner dimension
                s += a[i][k] * b[k][j]
            c[i][j] = s
    return c

# Example
A = [[1, 2, 3],
     [4, 5, 6]]         # 2 x 3

B = [[1, 0],
     [0, 1],
     [1, 1]]           # 3 x 2

C = matmul(A, B)
print(C)  # [[4.0, 5.0], [10.0, 11.0]]
snippets.explanation: This implementation follows the definition C(i,j) = sum over k of A(i,k) * B(k,j) and explicitly enforces the inner-dimension compatibility rule before computing the product.

snippets.title: Matrix product versus elementwise product in NumPy
snippets.code:
import numpy as np

A = np.array([[1.0, 2.0],
              [3.0, 4.0]])

B = np.array([[5.0, 6.0],
              [7.0, 8.0]])

mat_prod = A @ B        # true matrix multiplication
elt_prod = A * B        # elementwise multiplication

print("A @ B =\n", mat_prod)
print("A * B =\n", elt_prod)
snippets.explanation: In numerical libraries, the dedicated matrix-multiplication operator (like @ in Python) performs the row-by-column product, while plain * multiplies entries one by one; the two operations have different meanings and often different shape requirements.

items.title: Shape compatibility in AB
items.mermaid:
flowchart LR
  A["A (m x n)"] --> I["inner dimension n"]
  B["B (n x p)"] --> I
  A --> C["C = AB (m x p)"]
  B --> C
items.caption: Matrix multiplication AB is defined when columns(A) = rows(B); the result C keeps the outer dimensions m and p.

items.title: Matrix multiplication as composition
items.mermaid:
flowchart LR
  X["x in R^p"] --> Bmap["B: R^p -> R^n"] --> Amap["A: R^n -> R^m"] --> Y["y in R^m"]
  X -. "AB" .-> ABmap["AB: R^p -> R^m"] -.-> Y
items.caption: The product AB represents applying B first and then A, so AB is the composite linear map from the input space R^p to the output space R^m.

items.title: Matrices & operations
items.description: Matrix multiplication extends basic matrix operations such as addition, scalar multiplication and transpose by adding a way to compose linear maps.

items.title: Vectors & spaces
items.description: The row-by-column rule is defined in terms of dot products of vectors, so understanding matrix multiplication relies on the vector-space structure and inner products.

items.title: Norms & distances
items.description: Matrix multiplication can stretch or contract vectors; norms and distances let you quantify how much a given matrix product can amplify or shrink inputs.

items.title: Eigenvalues & eigenvectors
items.description: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors describe how repeated matrix multiplication scales and reorients vectors along special directions, especially for powers of a square matrix.

items.title: Linear and logistic regression with batched data
items.description: In linear and logistic regression, predictions for a whole batch of examples are computed as XW or XW + b, where X is a matrix of feature vectors and W is a weight matrix; matrix multiplication applies the same linear model to every example efficiently.

items.title: Dense layers in neural networks
items.description: Fully connected layers in neural networks use matrix multiplication to map activations from one layer to the next, with each forward and backward pass dominated by products of activation matrices and weight matrices.

items.title: Attention and transformer blocks
items.description: Transformers rely on large batched matrix multiplications for computing query-key dot products and combining values; attention scores and output sequences are produced almost entirely via chained matrix products.

items.title: Graph algorithms and Markov chains
items.description: Powers of an adjacency matrix count paths of fixed lengths in a graph, and powers of a stochastic matrix propagate probability distributions in Markov chains, making repeated matrix multiplication central to random walks and diffusion processes.

advantages: Captures composition of many linear steps as a single product, enabling concise algebra and aggressive optimisation of numerical code.
Highly optimised implementations on CPUs, GPUs and TPUs make matrix multiplication the fastest way to apply large linear models to batches of data.
Expresses operations on whole datasets or batches at once, aligning naturally with vectorised and parallel hardware.
disadvantages: Naive dense matrix multiplication is computationally expensive for large dimensions, often requiring algorithmic tricks, sparsity or factorisations to be practical.
Non-commutativity and strict shape rules make expressions order-sensitive; reordering or transposing matrices without care can silently change the meaning of a model.
Using matrix multiplication everywhere can hide fine-grained structure such as sparsity patterns, block structure or low rank that more specialised representations might exploit better.

items.myth: Two matrices can be multiplied only when they have exactly the same shape.
items.reality: AB is defined when the number of columns of A equals the number of rows of B; the outer dimensions can differ and become the shape of the product.
items.example: A 2 x 3 matrix can be multiplied by a 3 x 5 matrix to give a 2 x 5 matrix, even though none of the shapes are identical.

items.myth: Matrix multiplication is just elementwise multiplication with an extra sum at the end, so it behaves similarly to broadcasting.
items.reality: Each entry of AB depends on a full row of A and a full column of B, not on entries at the same index; elementwise multiplication ignores cross-row and cross-column interactions.
items.example: For C = AB with 2 x 2 matrices, C(0,0) = A(0,0)*B(0,0) + A(0,1)*B(1,0), which uses four entries, whereas an elementwise product D(0,0) = A(0,0)*B(0,0) uses only one.

items.myth: If AB is defined and BA is also defined, then AB and BA must be equal or at least similar in effect.
items.reality: Matrix multiplication is generally not commutative; even when both AB and BA exist, they can have different shapes and almost always different values.
items.example: In 2D geometry, a rotation matrix followed by a shear matrix gives a different transformation than shear followed by rotation; the corresponding matrices R and S satisfy RS != SR.

items.myth: Transposing a matrix does not really change anything important for multiplication; it just flips how you write the numbers.
items.reality: The transpose swaps rows and columns, changing the spaces a matrix maps between and which products are defined; many expressions are valid with A but invalid with AT, or vice versa.
items.example: If X has shape batch x features and W has shape features x outputs, then X @ W is defined and has shape batch x outputs, while XT @ W is undefined because features != batch.

items.title: Treating AB and BA as interchangeable
items.avoid: Assuming that the order of matrices in a product can be swapped without affecting the result as long as both products are defined.
items.prefer: Keep a fixed left-to-right order that reflects the sequence of linear transformations you intend to apply, and treat reordering as a change to the model.
items.why: Order encodes the pipeline of operations; swapping matrices usually represents a different composition and can even change the result's shape.
items.example: In a neural network, W2 @ W1 @ x applies the first layer W1 then the second layer W2; writing W1 @ W2 @ x inverts that order and generally produces different activations.

items.title: Using transposes purely to silence shape errors
items.avoid: Adding or removing transposes until the code runs, without reasoning about the geometric meaning of switching rows and columns.
items.prefer: Decide whether vectors are represented as rows or columns, annotate shapes, and only transpose when you explicitly intend to swap domain and codomain roles.
items.why: Random transposes can turn x into XT or W into WT and quietly alter which space each matrix acts on, producing outputs of the right shape but wrong semantics.
items.example: If X is batch x features and you mistakenly use XT in X.T @ W, you are effectively treating features as the batch dimension and mixing examples in unintended ways.

items.title: Forgetting that identity matrices are size-specific
items.avoid: Using a single identity matrix symbol I without tracking its dimension, as if it worked with any matrix in a product.
items.prefer: Attach dimensions to identity matrices, writing Im or In, and check that they have the same number of rows or columns as the matrices they are multiplying.
items.why: Identity matrices act as multiplicative identities only when their dimensions match the relevant side of the product; the wrong size identity leaves the algebra undefined.
items.example: For A in R^{3 x 5}, the right identity is I5 so that AI5 = A; using I3 on the right is not defined because columns(A) = 5 but rows(I3) = 3.

content.content:
Matrix multiplication emerged from the need to solve and compose large systems of linear equations efficiently.
Grouping coefficients into rectangular arrays made it possible to encode many equations at once and apply algebraic operations to the whole system instead of equation by equation.
In the 19th century, Cayley and others formalised matrices and the product rule that corresponds to composition of linear transformations, turning matrices into algebraic objects in their own right.
During the 20th century, the development of numerical linear algebra and high-performance computing elevated matrix multiplication to a central primitive in scientific computing, optimisation and eventually machine learning.
Modern generative models, from transformers to diffusion systems, are built on top of these ideas, relying on highly optimised matrix multiplication kernels on specialised hardware.

timeline.label: Early linear systems
timeline.description: Tabulating coefficients of simultaneous linear equations implicitly used matrix-like arrays, especially in astronomy and engineering.

timeline.label: 19th century matrix algebra
timeline.description: Cayley and contemporaries defined matrices and matrix multiplication explicitly, linking the product rule to composition of linear maps.

timeline.label: Numerical linear algebra era
timeline.description: Algorithms such as Gaussian elimination, LU and QR factorisation turned matrix multiplication and related operations into workhorses of scientific computation.

timeline.label: Modern ML and Gen AI
timeline.description: GPU-accelerated matrix multiplication became the core inner loop of training and inference for deep neural networks, including large generative models.

items.question: What is matrix multiplication in the context of linear algebra?
items.answer: Matrix multiplication is the operation that combines an m x n matrix A and an n x p matrix B into an m x p matrix AB, where each entry is computed as a dot product of a row of A with a column of B, representing the composition of two linear maps.

items.question: Why is matrix multiplication defined with the row-by-column rule instead of simple elementwise multiplication?
items.answer: The row-by-column rule ensures that matrix multiplication corresponds exactly to composing linear transformations, preserving key algebraic properties such as associativity and distributivity, whereas elementwise multiplication does not model composition of maps.

items.question: Who relies most heavily on matrix multiplication in practice?
items.answer: Practitioners such as machine learning engineers, data scientists, numerical analysts, physicists and computer graphics programmers rely heavily on matrix multiplication because it underpins batched linear models, simulation, optimisation and many core numerical algorithms.

items.question: Where does matrix multiplication appear inside modern ML and Gen AI systems?
items.answer: Matrix multiplication appears in linear and logistic regression, dense neural network layers, transformer attention blocks, recurrent networks, optimisers that use linear algebra routines and many preprocessing steps that apply linear transforms to whole datasets.

items.question: When can two matrices A and B be multiplied as AB?
items.answer: Two matrices A and B can be multiplied as AB exactly when the number of columns of A equals the number of rows of B; this shared size is the inner dimension, and it determines the length of the dot products that define each entry of AB.

items.question: How do you compute a specific entry C(i,j) of the product C = AB?
items.answer: To compute C(i,j), take row i of A and column j of B, multiply their corresponding components and sum the products, yielding C(i,j) = sum over k of A(i,k) * B(k,j), where k runs over the inner dimension.

items.question: What breaks if you try to multiply A of shape 2 x 3 and B of shape 4 x 2 as AB without changing either matrix?
items.answer: The product AB is undefined because the inner dimensions do not match: A has 3 columns while B has 4 rows, so you cannot form the required dot products between rows of A and columns of B without reshaping or replacing one of the matrices.

items.question: What breaks if you use elementwise multiplication X * W instead of X @ W in a linear model with design matrix X and weight matrix W?
items.answer: Elementwise multiplication ignores cross-feature interactions and requires shapes that broadcast correctly; each output entry depends on a single feature instead of a weighted sum across features, so the computation no longer represents the intended linear model.

items.question: What breaks if you randomly insert transposes into a chain of matrix multiplications until the code runs without shape errors?
items.answer: Random transposes can swap input and output spaces and reorder axes so that the resulting product no longer encodes the intended sequence of linear maps; the final shapes may look plausible but the numerical values can correspond to a completely different model or transformation.

items.scenario: You are implementing a dense layer in a neural network library and need to decide on the shape conventions for inputs and weights.
items.question: If you represent a batch of inputs as X with shape batch x d and the weight matrix as W with shape d x h, what is the shape of the product XW and how would you explain this choice to a teammate?
items.answer: With X of shape batch x d and W of shape d x h, the product XW has shape batch x h because the inner dimension d matches and the outer dimensions batch and h become the rows and columns of the result; this convention means each row of X is a feature vector and multiplying by W applies the same linear map from R^d to R^h to every example in the batch.

items.scenario: In a systems design interview, you are asked about optimising a pipeline that multiplies three large matrices in sequence.
items.question: Given matrices A (50 x 5), B (5 x 100) and C (100 x 2), which parenthesisation, (AB)C or A(BC), is cheaper using the standard dense matrix multiplication algorithm, and why?
items.answer: Computing (AB)C first forms AB with shape 50 x 100, costing 50*5*100 = 25,000 multiplications, and then multiplies this by C at a cost of 50*100*2 = 10,000, for a total of 35,000 multiplications. Computing A(BC) first forms BC with shape 5 x 2, costing 5*100*2 = 1,000 multiplications, and then multiplies A by this result at a cost of 50*5*2 = 500, for a total of 1,500 multiplications. A(BC) is therefore much cheaper because it keeps the intermediate dimensions small.

items.scenario: During a whiteboard interview, you are asked to relate matrix multiplication to composing functions.
items.question: How would you explain matrix multiplication as composition of linear transformations to someone who is comfortable with code but less familiar with linear algebra?
items.answer: You can think of each matrix as a function that takes a vector as input and returns another vector as output, subject to linearity constraints. If B maps input vectors to some intermediate representation and A maps that intermediate representation to outputs, then the composed function that applies B and then A is represented by the single matrix product AB. In code terms, AB plays the role of a composed function like f(g(x)), but expressed as a single matrix multiply that you can apply efficiently to many inputs at once.

items.question: If A has shape 3 x 4 and B has shape 4 x 2, what is the shape of AB?
items.explanation: The inner dimensions 4 and 4 match, so AB is defined and has shape rows(A) x columns(B) = 3 x 2.

items.question: Which statement about matrix multiplication is always true for compatible matrices A, B and C?
options: AB is defined only when A and B have the same shape
Each entry of AB depends on a single entry of A and a single entry of B
items.explanation: Matrix multiplication is associative, so (AB)C and A(BC) are always equal when the products are defined; it is generally not commutative, does not require identical shapes and each entry depends on a full row and column.

items.question: Let A be an m x n matrix and In be the n x n identity matrix. Which equality holds whenever the product is defined?
options: InA is undefined for non-square A
items.explanation: The identity matrix In acts as a multiplicative identity on the right for any m x n matrix A with n columns, so multiplying by In on the right leaves A unchanged.

items.question: Suppose X has shape batch x d, W1 has shape d x h and W2 has shape h x k. What is the most efficient way to compute XW1W2?
items.explanation: Computing W1W2 first costs d*h*k multiplications and produces a d x k matrix; multiplying X by that costs batch*d*k. Computing (XW1)W2 first costs batch*d*h and then batch*h*k, which is more expensive when batch is large. Both parenthesisations return the same result but X(W1W2) is cheaper in typical ML settings where batch is big.

criteria.label: Determine at a glance whether AB is defined and what shape it has for small matrices A and B.
criteria.example: Given A of shape 4 x 3 and B of shape 3 x 5, you immediately state that AB exists and has shape 4 x 5, while BA is undefined because 5 != 4.

criteria.label: Compute small matrix products by hand using the row-by-column rule without losing track of indices.
criteria.example: For 2 x 2 matrices A and B with simple integer entries, you can fill in all four entries of AB and explain each as a dot product of a row of A with a column of B.

criteria.label: Explain matrix multiplication as composition of linear maps and use this view to reason about chains of transformations.
criteria.example: In 2D, you can describe a rotation matrix R and a scaling matrix S, write down RS and SR, and explain how RS and SR represent different orderings of rotation and scaling that act differently on test vectors.

criteria.label: Choose an efficient parenthesisation for long products of matrices based on dimensions and estimate the resulting operation counts.
criteria.example: Given A (1000 x 10), B (10 x 100) and C (100 x 5), you select A(BC) rather than (AB)C and justify the choice by showing that A(BC) requires orders of magnitude fewer scalar multiplications.

criteria.label: Diagnose and fix shape and transpose bugs in ML code that uses batched matrix multiplication.
criteria.example: When a transformer attention block crashes with a shape mismatch, you can trace through query, key and value shapes, identify which transpose or reshape is inconsistent, and correct it so that all matrix multiplications reflect the intended batched dot products.

notes: This manifest targets the first pending queue entry linear-algebra--matrix-multiplication under math-classical-ml > core-math > linear-algebra. Included sections are explanation, examples, code, diagrams, synonyms, applications, tradeoffs, mistakes, misconceptions, origins, question-patterns, interview-questions, exam-prep and mastery to satisfy required coverage for a foundational topic. Omitted connections, best-practices, materials, projects, scenario-questions, case-studies, images and charts because the chosen sections already cover definitions, practice, conceptual pitfalls, and self-assessment within the recommended 10–14 section range, and Mermaid diagrams are sufficient for visuals without additional static images.
